verses.text: The ark set in its tent, David offers sacrifices, blesses the people, and gives every man and woman bread, meat, and wine. Worship overflows into open-handed generosity. A heart full of God becomes a hand open to others.

The ark set in its tent, David offers sacrifices, blesses the people, and gives every man and woman bread, meat, and wine. Worship overflows into open-handed generosity. A heart full of God becomes a hand open to others.

The ark set in its tent, David offers sacrifices, blesses the people, and gives every man and woman bread, meat, and wine. Worship overflows into open-handed generosity. A heart full of God becomes a hand open to others.

David appoints Levites to minister before the ark, 'to record, and to thank and praise the LORD,' with trumpets continually. Thanksgiving is made a settled, ongoing ministry. God is to be praised not in bursts but day after day.

David appoints Levites to minister before the ark, 'to record, and to thank and praise the LORD,' with trumpets continually. Thanksgiving is made a settled, ongoing ministry. God is to be praised not in bursts but day after day.

David appoints Levites to minister before the ark, 'to record, and to thank and praise the LORD,' with trumpets continually. Thanksgiving is made a settled, ongoing ministry. God is to be praised not in bursts but day after day.

David's psalm begins: give thanks, call on His name, sing, and 'let the heart of them rejoice that seek the LORD.' The worshiping life is a seeking life. Those who seek God find their joy in the seeking itself.

'Seek the LORD and his strength, seek his face continually,' remembering His wondrous works, O seed of Israel His chosen. The call is to constant seeking. The settled habit of God's people is to look continually to His face.

'Seek the LORD and his strength, seek his face continually,' remembering His wondrous works, O seed of Israel His chosen. The call is to constant seeking. The settled habit of God's people is to look continually to His face.

'Seek the LORD and his strength, seek his face continually,' remembering His wondrous works, O seed of Israel His chosen. The call is to constant seeking. The settled habit of God's people is to look continually to His face.

The psalm recalls God's everlasting covenant with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, His word commanded to a thousand generations. God's promises do not expire. What He swore to the fathers He keeps faithfully to their children's children.

God gave them the land when they were few and wandering, and 'suffered no man to do them wrong... Touch not mine anointed.' He guarded His own when they were weak. The covenant-keeping God shields His people, however small, from harm.

'Sing unto the LORD, all the earth... Declare his glory among the heathen,' for great is the LORD and the gods of the nations are idols. The praise of God is for every people. He alone is God, worthy of the worship of all the earth.

'Sing unto the LORD, all the earth... Declare his glory among the heathen,' for great is the LORD and the gods of the nations are idols. The praise of God is for every people. He alone is God, worthy of the worship of all the earth.

Give the LORD the glory due His name, bring an offering, and 'worship the LORD in the beauty of holiness.' Reverent, holy worship is His due. There is a beauty in true worship the world's idolatry can never match.

'Let the heavens be glad... let men say among the nations, The LORD reigneth'; the fields and trees rejoice, for He comes to judge the earth. All creation is called to praise His reign. The kingship of God is the joy of heaven and earth.

'Let the heavens be glad... let men say among the nations, The LORD reigneth'; the fields and trees rejoice, for He comes to judge the earth. All creation is called to praise His reign. The kingship of God is the joy of heaven and earth.

'Let the heavens be glad... let men say among the nations, The LORD reigneth'; the fields and trees rejoice, for He comes to judge the earth. All creation is called to praise His reign. The kingship of God is the joy of heaven and earth.

'O give thanks unto the LORD; for he is good; for his mercy endureth for ever,' and the prayer rises, 'save us... gather us together' — the cry of a people longing for restoration. The ground of all thanks is His unfailing, everlasting mercy.

David leaves Asaph to minister before the ark continually, and Zadok at Gibeon to offer burnt offerings morning and evening, 'according to all that is written in the law.' Worship is to be both heartfelt and obedient, daily and ordered as God commanded.

Heman and Jeduthun give thanks because 'his mercy endureth for ever,' and then David returns 'to bless his house.' Public devotion does not crowd out the home. The man who ordered the nation's worship went home to bless his own family too.
